Math401Math 401, Summer 2025: Freiwald research project notesMath 401, Topic 2: Finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces

Math401 Topic 2: Finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces

Recall the complex number is a tuple of two real numbers, z=(a,b)z=(a,b)z=(a,b) with addition and multiplication defined by

(a,b)+(c,d)=(a+c,b+d)(a,b)+(c,d)=(a+c,b+d)(a,b)+(c,d)=(a+c,b+d) (a,b)⋅(c,d)=(ac−bd,ad+bc)(a,b)\cdot(c,d)=(ac-bd,ad+bc)(a,b)⋅(c,d)=(ac−bd,ad+bc)

or in polar form,

z=reiθ=r(cos⁡θ+isin⁡θ)z=re^{i\theta}=r(\cos\theta+i\sin\theta)z=reiθ=r(cosθ+isinθ)

where r=a2+b2=zz‾r=\sqrt{a^2+b^2}=\sqrt{z\overline{z}}r=a2+b2​=zz​ and θ=tan⁡−1(b/a)\theta=\tan^{-1}(b/a)θ=tan−1(b/a).

The complex conjugate of zzz is z‾=(a,−b)\overline{z}=(a,-b)z=(a,−b).

Section 1: Finite-dimensional Complex Vector Spaces

Here, we use the field C\mathbb{C}C of complex numbers. or the field R\mathbb{R}R of real numbers as the field F\mathbb{F}F we are going to encounter.

Definition of vector space

A vector space V\mathscr{V}V over a field F\mathbb{F}F is a set equipped with an addition and a scalar multiplication, satisfying the following axioms:

  1. Addition is associative and commutative. For all u,v,w∈Vu,v,w\in \mathscr{V}u,v,w∈V,

Associativity:

(u+v)+w=u+(v+w)(u+v)+w=u+(v+w)(u+v)+w=u+(v+w)

Commutativity:

u+v=v+uu+v=v+uu+v=v+u

  1. Additive identity: There exists an element 0∈V0\in \mathscr{V}0∈V such that v+0=vv+0=vv+0=v for all v∈Vv\in \mathscr{V}v∈V.

  2. Additive inverse: For each v∈Vv\in \mathscr{V}v∈V, there exists an element −v∈V-v\in \mathscr{V}−v∈V such that v+(−v)=0v+(-v)=0v+(−v)=0.

  3. Multiplicative identity: There exists an element 1∈F1\in \mathbb{F}1∈F such that v⋅1=vv\cdot 1=vv⋅1=v for all v∈Vv\in \mathscr{V}v∈V.

  4. Multiplicative inverse: For each v∈Vv\in \mathscr{V}v∈V and c∈Fc\in \mathbb{F}c∈F, there exists an element c−1∈Fc^{-1}\in \mathbb{F}c−1∈F such that v⋅c−1=1v\cdot c^{-1}=1v⋅c−1=1.

  5. Distributivity: For all u,v∈Vu,v\in \mathscr{V}u,v∈V and c,d∈Fc,d\in \mathbb{F}c,d∈F,

c(u+v)=cu+cvc(u+v)=cu+cvc(u+v)=cu+cv

A vector is an ordered pair of elements over the field F\mathbb{F}F.

If we consider F=Cn\mathbb{F}=\mathbb{C}^nF=Cn, n∈Nn\in \mathbb{N}n∈N, then u=(a1,a2,⋯ ,an),v=(b1,b2,⋯ ,bn)∈Cnu=(a_1,a_2,\cdots,a_n), v=(b_1,b_2,\cdots,b_n)\in \mathbb{C}^nu=(a1​,a2​,⋯,an​),v=(b1​,b2​,⋯,bn​)∈Cn are vectors.

The addition and scalar multiplication are defined by

u+v=(a1+b1,a2+b2,⋯ ,an+bn)u+v=(a_1+b_1,a_2+b_2,\cdots,a_n+b_n)u+v=(a1​+b1​,a2​+b2​,⋯,an​+bn​) cu=(ca1,ca2,⋯ ,can)cu=(ca_1,ca_2,\cdots,ca_n)cu=(ca1​,ca2​,⋯,can​)

c∈Cc\in \mathbb{C}c∈C.

The matrix transpose is defined by

u⊤=(a1,a2,⋯ ,an)⊤=(a1a2⋮an)u^\top=(a_1,a_2,\cdots,a_n)^\top=\begin{pmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_n \end{pmatrix}u⊤=(a1​,a2​,⋯,an​)⊤=​a1​a2​⋮an​​​

The complex conjugate transpose is defined by

u∗=(a1,a2,⋯ ,an)∗=(a1‾a2‾⋮an‾)u^*=(a_1,a_2,\cdots,a_n)^*=\begin{pmatrix} \overline{a_1} \\ \overline{a_2} \\ \vdots \\ \overline{a_n} \end{pmatrix}u∗=(a1​,a2​,⋯,an​)∗=​a1​​a2​​⋮an​​​​

In physics, the complex conjugate is sometimes denoted by z∗z^*z∗ instead of z‾\overline{z}z. The complex conjugate transpose is sometimes denoted by u†u^\daggeru† instead of u∗u^*u∗.

Hermitian inner product and norms

On Cn\mathbb{C}^nCn, the Hermitian inner product is defined by

⟨u,v⟩=∑i=1nui‾vi\langle u,v\rangle=\sum_{i=1}^n \overline{u_i}v_i⟨u,v⟩=i=1∑n​ui​​vi​

The norm is defined by

∥u∥=⟨u,u⟩\|u\|=\sqrt{\langle u,u\rangle}∥u∥=⟨u,u⟩​

Definition of Inner product

Let H\mathscr{H}H be a complex vector space. An inner product on H\mathscr{H}H is a function ⟨⋅,⋅⟩:H×H→C\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle: \mathscr{H}\times \mathscr{H}\to \mathbb{C}⟨⋅,⋅⟩:H×H→C satisfying the following axioms:

  1. For each u∈Hu\in \mathscr{H}u∈H, v↦⟨u,v⟩v\mapsto \langle u,v\ranglev↦⟨u,v⟩ is a linear map.
⟨u,av+bw⟩=a⟨u,v⟩+b⟨u,w⟩\langle u,av+bw\rangle=a\langle u,v\rangle+b\langle u,w\rangle⟨u,av+bw⟩=a⟨u,v⟩+b⟨u,w⟩

For all u,v,w∈Hu,v,w\in \mathscr{H}u,v,w∈H and a,b∈Ca,b\in \mathbb{C}a,b∈C.

  1. For all u,v∈Hu,v\in \mathscr{H}u,v∈H, ⟨u,v⟩=⟨v,u⟩‾\langle u,v\rangle=\overline{\langle v,u\rangle}⟨u,v⟩=⟨v,u⟩​.

u↦⟨u,v⟩u\mapsto \langle u,v\rangleu↦⟨u,v⟩ is a conjugate linear map.

  1. ⟨u,u⟩≥0\langle u,u\rangle\geq 0⟨u,u⟩≥0 and ⟨u,u⟩=0\langle u,u\rangle=0⟨u,u⟩=0 if and only if u=0u=0u=0.

Definition of norm

Let H\mathscr{H}H be a complex vector space. A norm on H\mathscr{H}H is a function ∥⋅∥:H→R\|\cdot\|: \mathscr{H}\to \mathbb{R}∥⋅∥:H→R satisfying the following axioms:

  1. For all u∈Hu\in \mathscr{H}u∈H, ∥u∥≥0\|u\|\geq 0∥u∥≥0 and ∥u∥=0\|u\|=0∥u∥=0 if and only if u=0u=0u=0.

  2. For all u∈Hu\in \mathscr{H}u∈H and c∈Cc\in \mathbb{C}c∈C, ∥cu∥=∣c∣∥u∥\|cu\|=|c|\|u\|∥cu∥=∣c∣∥u∥.

  3. Triangle inequality: For all u,v∈Hu,v\in \mathscr{H}u,v∈H, ∥u+v∥≤∥u∥+∥v∥\|u+v\|\leq \|u\|+\|v\|∥u+v∥≤∥u∥+∥v∥.

Definition of inner product space

A complex vector space H\mathscr{H}H with an inner product is called a Hilbert space.

Cauchy-Schwarz inequality

For all u,v∈Hu,v\in \mathscr{H}u,v∈H,

∣⟨u,v⟩∣≤∥u∥∥v∥|\langle u,v\rangle|\leq \|u\|\|v\|∣⟨u,v⟩∣≤∥u∥∥v∥

Parallelogram law

For all u,v∈Hu,v\in \mathscr{H}u,v∈H,

∥u+v∥2+∥u−v∥2=2(∥u∥2+∥v∥2)\|u+v\|^2+\|u-v\|^2=2(\|u\|^2+\|v\|^2)∥u+v∥2+∥u−v∥2=2(∥u∥2+∥v∥2)

Polarization identity

For all u,v∈Hu,v\in \mathscr{H}u,v∈H,

⟨u,v⟩=14(∥u+v∥2−∥u−v∥2+i∥u+iv∥2−i∥u−iv∥2)\langle u,v\rangle=\frac{1}{4}(\|u+v\|^2-\|u-v\|^2+i\|u+iv\|^2-i\|u-iv\|^2)⟨u,v⟩=41​(∥u+v∥2−∥u−v∥2+i∥u+iv∥2−i∥u−iv∥2)

Additional definitions

Let u,v∈Hu,v\in \mathscr{H}u,v∈H.

∥v∥\|v\|∥v∥ is the length of vvv.

vvv is a unit vector if ∥v∥=1\|v\|=1∥v∥=1.

u,vu,vu,v are orthogonal if ⟨u,v⟩=0\langle u,v\rangle=0⟨u,v⟩=0.

Definition of orthonormal basis

A set of vectors {e1,e2,⋯ ,en}\{e_1,e_2,\cdots,e_n\}{e1​,e2​,⋯,en​} in a Hilbert space H\mathscr{H}H is called an orthonormal basis if

  1. ⟨ei,ej⟩=δij\langle e_i,e_j\rangle=\delta_{ij}⟨ei​,ej​⟩=δij​ for all i,j∈{1,2,⋯ ,n}i,j\in \{1,2,\cdots,n\}i,j∈{1,2,⋯,n}.
δij={1if i=j0if i≠j\delta_{ij}=\begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } i=j \\ 0 & \text{if } i\neq j \end{cases}δij​={10​if i=jif i=j​
  1. n=dim⁡Hn=\dim \mathscr{H}n=dimH.

Subspaces and orthonormal bases

Definition of subspace

A subset W\mathscr{W}W of a vector space V\mathscr{V}V is a subspace if it is closed under addition and scalar multiplication.

Definition of orthogonal complement

Let EEE be a subset of a Hilbert space H\mathscr{H}H. The orthogonal complement of EEE is the set of all vectors in H\mathscr{H}H that are orthogonal to every vector in EEE.

E⊥={v∈H:⟨v,w⟩=0 for all w∈E}E^\perp=\{v\in \mathscr{H}: \langle v,w\rangle=0 \text{ for all } w\in E\}E⊥={v∈H:⟨v,w⟩=0 for all w∈E}

Definition of orthogonal projection

Let EEE be a mmm-dimensional subspace of a Hilbert space H\mathscr{H}H. An orthogonal projection of EEE is a linear map PE:H→EP_E: \mathscr{H}\to EPE​:H→E

PE(v)=∑i=1m⟨v,ei⟩eiP_E(v)=\sum_{i=1}^m \langle v,e_i\rangle e_iPE​(v)=i=1∑m​⟨v,ei​⟩ei​

Definition of orthonormal direct sum

A inner product space H\mathscr{H}H is the direct sum of E1,E2,⋯ ,EnE_1,E_2,\cdots,E_nE1​,E2​,⋯,En​ if

H=E1⊕E2⊕⋯⊕En\mathscr{H}=E_1\oplus E_2\oplus \cdots \oplus E_nH=E1​⊕E2​⊕⋯⊕En​

and Ei∩Ej={0}E_i\cap E_j=\{0\}Ei​∩Ej​={0} for all i≠ji\neq ji=j.

That is, ∀v∈H\forall v\in \mathscr{H}∀v∈H, there exists a unique vi∈Eiv_i\in E_ivi​∈Ei​ such that v=v1+v2+⋯+vnv=v_1+v_2+\cdots+v_nv=v1​+v2​+⋯+vn​.

Definition of meet and join of subspaces

Let EEE and FFF be two subspaces of a Hilbert space H\mathscr{H}H. The meet of EEE and FFF is the subspace H\mathscr{H}H such that

E∧F=E∩FE\land F=E\cap FE∧F=E∩F

The join of EEE and FFF is the subspace H\mathscr{H}H such that

E∨F={u+v:u∈E,v∈F}E\lor F=\{u+v: u\in E, v\in F\}E∨F={u+v:u∈E,v∈F}

Null space and range

Definition of null space

Let AAA be a linear map from a vector space V\mathscr{V}V to a vector space W\mathscr{W}W. The null space of AAA is the set of all vectors in V\mathscr{V}V that are mapped to the zero vector in W\mathscr{W}W.

Null(A)={v∈V:Av=0}\text{Null}(A)=\{v\in \mathscr{V}: Av=0\}Null(A)={v∈V:Av=0}

Definition of range

Let AAA be a linear map from a vector space V\mathscr{V}V to a vector space W\mathscr{W}W. The range of AAA is the set of all vectors in W\mathscr{W}W that are mapped from V\mathscr{V}V.

Range(A)={w∈W:∃v∈V,Av=w}\text{Range}(A)=\{w\in \mathscr{W}: \exists v\in \mathscr{V}, Av=w\}Range(A)={w∈W:∃v∈V,Av=w}

Dual spaces and adjoints of linear maps

Definition of linear map

A linear map T:V→WT: \mathscr{V}\to \mathscr{W}T:V→W is a function that satisfies the following axioms:

  1. Additivity: For all u,v∈Vu,v\in \mathscr{V}u,v∈V and a,b∈Fa,b\in \mathbb{F}a,b∈F,
T(au+bv)=aT(u)+bT(v)T(au+bv)=aT(u)+bT(v)T(au+bv)=aT(u)+bT(v)
  1. Homogeneity: For all u∈Vu\in \mathscr{V}u∈V and a∈Fa\in \mathbb{F}a∈F,
T(au)=aT(u)T(au)=aT(u)T(au)=aT(u)

Definition of linear functionals

A linear functional f:V→Ff: \mathscr{V}\to \mathbb{F}f:V→F is a linear map from V\mathscr{V}V to F\mathbb{F}F.

Here, F\mathbb{F}F is the field of complex numbers.

Definition of dual space

Let V\mathscr{V}V be a vector space over a field F\mathbb{F}F. The dual space of V\mathscr{V}V is the set of all linear functionals on V\mathscr{V}V.

V∗={f:V→F:f is linear}\mathscr{V}^*=\{f:\mathscr{V}\to \mathbb{F}: f\text{ is linear}\}V∗={f:V→F:f is linear}

If H\mathscr{H}H is a finite-dimensional Hilbert space, then H∗\mathscr{H}^*H∗ is isomorphic to H\mathscr{H}H.

Note v∈H↦⟨v,⋅⟩∈H∗v\in \mathscr{H}\mapsto \langle v,\cdot\rangle\in \mathscr{H}^*v∈H↦⟨v,⋅⟩∈H∗ is a conjugate linear isomorphism.

Definition of adjoint of a linear map

Let T:V→WT: \mathscr{V}\to \mathscr{W}T:V→W be a linear map. The adjoint of TTT is the linear map T∗:W→VT^*: \mathscr{W}\to \mathscr{V}T∗:W→V such that

⟨Tv,w⟩=⟨v,T∗w⟩\langle Tv,w\rangle=\langle v,T^*w\rangle⟨Tv,w⟩=⟨v,T∗w⟩

for all v∈Vv\in \mathscr{V}v∈V and w∈Ww\in \mathscr{W}w∈W.

Definition of self-adjoint operators

A linear operator T:V→VT: \mathscr{V}\to \mathscr{V}T:V→V is self-adjoint if T∗=TT^*=TT∗=T.

Definition of unitary operators

A linear map T:V→VT: \mathscr{V}\to \mathscr{V}T:V→V is unitary if T∗T=TT∗=IT^*T=TT^*=IT∗T=TT∗=I.

Dirac’s bra-ket notation

Definition of bra and ket

Let H\mathscr{H}H be a Hilbert space. The bra-ket notation is a notation for vectors in H\mathscr{H}H.

⟨v∣w⟩\langle v|w\rangle⟨v∣w⟩

is the inner product of vvv and www. That is, ⟨v∣w⟩:H→C\langle v|w\rangle: \mathscr{H}\to \mathbb{C}⟨v∣w⟩:H→C is a linear functional satisfying the property of inner product.

∣v⟩|v\rangle∣v⟩

is the vector (or linear map) vvv.

∣u⟩⟨v∣|u\rangle\langle v|∣u⟩⟨v∣

is a linear map from H\mathscr{H}H to H\mathscr{H}H.

The spectral theorem for self-adjoint operators

Spectral theorem for self-adjoint operators

Definition of spectral theorem

Let H\mathscr{H}H be a Hilbert space. A self-adjoint operator T:H→HT: \mathscr{H}\to \mathscr{H}T:H→H is a linear operator that is equal to its adjoint.

Then all the eigenvalues of TTT are real and there exists an orthonormal basis of H\mathscr{H}H consisting of eigenvectors of TTT.

Definition of spectrum

The spectrum of a linear operator on finite-dimensional Hilbert space T:H→HT: \mathscr{H}\to \mathscr{H}T:H→H is the set of all distinct eigenvalues of TTT.

sp⁡(T)={λ:λ is an eigenvalue of T}⊂C\operatorname{sp}(T)=\{\lambda: \lambda\text{ is an eigenvalue of } T\}\subset \mathbb{C}sp(T)={λ:λ is an eigenvalue of T}⊂C

Definition of Eigenspace

If λ\lambdaλ is an eigenvalue of TTT, the eigenspace of TTT corresponding to λ\lambdaλ is the set of all eigenvectors of TTT corresponding to λ\lambdaλ.

Eλ(T)={v∈H:Tv=λv}E_\lambda(T)=\{v\in \mathscr{H}: Tv=\lambda v\}Eλ​(T)={v∈H:Tv=λv}

We denote Pλ(T):H→Eλ(T)P_\lambda(T):\mathscr{H}\to E_\lambda(T)Pλ​(T):H→Eλ​(T) the orthogonal projection onto Eλ(T)E_\lambda(T)Eλ​(T).

Definition of Operator norm

The operator norm of a linear operator T:H→HT: \mathscr{H}\to \mathscr{H}T:H→H is the largest eigenvalue of TTT.

∥T∥=max⁡∥v∥=1∥Tv∥\|T\|=\max_{\|v\|=1} \|Tv\|∥T∥=∥v∥=1max​∥Tv∥

We say TTT is bounded if ∥T∥<∞\|T\|<\infty∥T∥<∞.

We denote B(H)B(\mathscr{H})B(H) the set of all bounded linear operators on H\mathscr{H}H.

Partial trace

Definition of trace

Let TTT be a linear operator on H\mathscr{H}H, (e1,e2,⋯ ,en)(e_1,e_2,\cdots,e_n)(e1​,e2​,⋯,en​) be a basis of H\mathscr{H}H and (ϵ1,ϵ2,⋯ ,ϵn)(\epsilon_1,\epsilon_2,\cdots,\epsilon_n)(ϵ1​,ϵ2​,⋯,ϵn​) be a basis of dual space H∗\mathscr{H}^*H∗. Then the trace of TTT is defined by

Tr⁡(T)=∑i=1nϵi(T(ei))=∑i=1n⟨ei,T(ei)⟩\operatorname{Tr}(T)=\sum_{i=1}^n \epsilon_i(T(e_i))=\sum_{i=1}^n \langle e_i,T(e_i)\rangleTr(T)=i=1∑n​ϵi​(T(ei​))=i=1∑n​⟨ei​,T(ei​)⟩

This is equivalent to the sum of the diagonal elements of TTT.

Note, I changed the order of the definitions for the trace to pack similar concepts together. Check the rest of the section defining the partial trace by viewing the tensor product section  first, and return to this section after reading the tensor product of linear operators.

Definition of partial trace

Let TTT be a linear operator on H=A⊗B\mathscr{H}=\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B}H=A⊗B, where A\mathscr{A}A and B\mathscr{B}B are finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces.

An operator TTT on H=A⊗B\mathscr{H}=\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B}H=A⊗B can be written as (by the definition of tensor product of linear operators )

T=∑i=1naiAi⊗BiT=\sum_{i=1}^n a_i A_i\otimes B_iT=i=1∑n​ai​Ai​⊗Bi​

where AiA_iAi​ is a linear operator on A\mathscr{A}A and BiB_iBi​ is a linear operator on B\mathscr{B}B.

The B\mathscr{B}B-partial trace of TTT (Tr⁡B(T):L(A⊗B)→L(A)\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathscr{B}}(T):\mathcal{L}(\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B})\to \mathcal{L}(\mathscr{A})TrB​(T):L(A⊗B)→L(A)) is the linear operator on A\mathscr{A}A defined by

Tr⁡B(T)=∑i=1naiTr⁡(Bi)Ai\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathscr{B}}(T)=\sum_{i=1}^n a_i \operatorname{Tr}(B_i) A_iTrB​(T)=i=1∑n​ai​Tr(Bi​)Ai​

Or we can define the map Lv:A→A⊗BL_v: \mathscr{A}\to \mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B}Lv​:A→A⊗B by

Lv(u)=u⊗vL_v(u)=u\otimes vLv​(u)=u⊗v

Note that ⟨u,Lv∗(u′)⊗v′⟩=⟨u,u′⟩⟨v,v′⟩=⟨u⊗v,u′⊗v′⟩=⟨Lv(u),u′⊗v′⟩\langle u,L_v^*(u')\otimes v'\rangle=\langle u,u'\rangle \langle v,v'\rangle=\langle u\otimes v,u'\otimes v'\rangle=\langle L_v(u),u'\otimes v'\rangle⟨u,Lv∗​(u′)⊗v′⟩=⟨u,u′⟩⟨v,v′⟩=⟨u⊗v,u′⊗v′⟩=⟨Lv​(u),u′⊗v′⟩.

Therefore, Lv∗∑juj⊗vj=∑j⟨v,vj⟩ujL_v^*\sum_{j} u_j\otimes v_j=\sum_{j} \langle v,v_j\rangle u_jLv∗​∑j​uj​⊗vj​=∑j​⟨v,vj​⟩uj​.

Then the partial trace of TTT can also be defined by

Let {vj}\{v_j\}{vj​} be a set of orthonormal basis of B\mathscr{B}B.

Tr⁡B(T)=∑jLvj∗(T)Lvj\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathscr{B}}(T)=\sum_{j} L^*_{v_j}(T)L_{v_j}TrB​(T)=j∑​Lvj​∗​(T)Lvj​​

Definition of partial trace with respect to a state

Let TTT be a linear operator on H=A⊗B\mathscr{H}=\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B}H=A⊗B, where A\mathscr{A}A and B\mathscr{B}B are finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces.

Let ρ\rhoρ be a state on B\mathscr{B}B consisting of orthonormal basis {vj}\{v_j\}{vj​} and eigenvalue {λj}\{\lambda_j\}{λj​}.

The partial trace of TTT with respect to ρ\rhoρ is the linear operator on A\mathscr{A}A defined by

Tr⁡A(T)=∑jλjLvj∗(T)Lvj\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathscr{A}}(T)=\sum_{j} \lambda_j L^*_{v_j}(T)L_{v_j}TrA​(T)=j∑​λj​Lvj​∗​(T)Lvj​​

Space of Bounded Linear Operators

Recall the trace of a matrix is the sum of its diagonal elements.

Hilbert-Schmidt inner product

Let T,S∈B(H)T,S\in B(\mathscr{H})T,S∈B(H). The Hilbert-Schmidt inner product of TTT and SSS is defined by

⟨T,S⟩=Tr⁡(T∗S)\langle T,S\rangle=\operatorname{Tr}(T^*S)⟨T,S⟩=Tr(T∗S)

Note here, T∗T^*T∗ is the complex conjugate transpose of TTT.

If we introduce the basis {ei}\{e_i\}{ei​} in H\mathscr{H}H, then we can write the the space of bounded linear operators as n×nn\times nn×n complex-valued matrices Mn(C)M_n(\mathbb{C})Mn​(C).

For T=(aij)T=(a_{ij})T=(aij​), S=(bij)S=(b_{ij})S=(bij​), we have

Tr⁡(A∗B)=∑i=1n∑j=1naij‾bij\operatorname{Tr}(A^*B)=\sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^n \overline{a_{ij}}b_{ij}Tr(A∗B)=i=1∑n​j=1∑n​aij​​bij​

The inner product is the standard Hermitian inner product in Cn×n\mathbb{C}^{n\times n}Cn×n.

Definition of Hilbert-Schmidt norm (also called Frobenius norm)

The Hilbert-Schmidt norm of a linear operator T:H→HT: \mathscr{H}\to \mathscr{H}T:H→H is defined by

∥T∥=∑i=1n∑j=1n∣aij∣2\|T\|=\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^n |a_{ij}|^2}∥T∥=i=1∑n​j=1∑n​∣aij​∣2​

The trace of operator does not depend on the basis. 

Tensor products of finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces

Let X=X1×X2×⋯×XnX=X_1\times X_2\times \cdots \times X_nX=X1​×X2​×⋯×Xn​ be a Cartesian product of nnn sets.

Let x=(x1,x2,⋯ ,xn)x=(x_1,x_2,\cdots,x_n)x=(x1​,x2​,⋯,xn​) be a vector in XXX. xj∈Xjx_j\in X_jxj​∈Xj​ for j=1,2,⋯ ,nj=1,2,\cdots,nj=1,2,⋯,n.

Let a∈Xja\in X_ja∈Xj​ for j=1,2,⋯ ,nj=1,2,\cdots,nj=1,2,⋯,n.

Let’s denote the space of all functions from XXX to C\mathbb{C}C by H\mathscr{H}H and the space of all functions from XjX_jXj​ to C\mathbb{C}C by Hj\mathscr{H}_jHj​.

ϵa(j)(xj)={1if xj=a0if xj≠a\epsilon_{a}^{(j)}(x_j)=\begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x_j=a \\ 0 & \text{if } x_j\neq a \end{cases}ϵa(j)​(xj​)={10​if xj​=aif xj​=a​

Then we can define a basis of Hj\mathscr{H}_jHj​ by {ϵa(j)(xj)}a∈Xj\{\epsilon_{a}^{(j)}(x_j)\}_{a\in X_j}{ϵa(j)​(xj​)}a∈Xj​​.

Any function f:Xj→Cf:X_j\to \mathbb{C}f:Xj​→C can be written as a linear combination of the basis vectors.

f(xj)=∑a∈Xjf(a)ϵa(j)(xj)f(x_j)=\sum_{a\in X_j} f(a)\epsilon_{a}^{(j)}(x_j)f(xj​)=a∈Xj​∑​f(a)ϵa(j)​(xj​)

Proof

Note that a function is a map for all elements in the domain.

For each a∈Xja\in X_ja∈Xj​, ϵa(j)(xj)=1\epsilon_{a}^{(j)}(x_j)=1ϵa(j)​(xj​)=1 if xj=ax_j=axj​=a and 000 otherwise. So

f(xj)=∑a∈Xjf(a)ϵa(j)(xj)=f(xj)f(x_j)=\sum_{a\in X_j} f(a)\epsilon_{a}^{(j)}(x_j)=f(x_j)f(xj​)=a∈Xj​∑​f(a)ϵa(j)​(xj​)=f(xj​)

Now, let a=(a1,a2,⋯ ,an)a=(a_1,a_2,\cdots,a_n)a=(a1​,a2​,⋯,an​) be a vector in XXX, and x=(x1,x2,⋯ ,xn)x=(x_1,x_2,\cdots,x_n)x=(x1​,x2​,⋯,xn​) be a vector in XXX. Note that aj,xj∈Xja_j,x_j\in X_jaj​,xj​∈Xj​ for j=1,2,⋯ ,nj=1,2,\cdots,nj=1,2,⋯,n.

Define

ϵa(x)=∏j=1nϵaj(j)(xj)={1if aj=xj for all j=1,2,⋯ ,n0otherwise\epsilon_a(x)=\prod_{j=1}^n \epsilon_{a_j}^{(j)}(x_j)=\begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a_j=x_j \text{ for all } j=1,2,\cdots,n \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}ϵa​(x)=j=1∏n​ϵaj​(j)​(xj​)={10​if aj​=xj​ for all j=1,2,⋯,notherwise​

Then we can define a basis of H\mathscr{H}H by {ϵa}a∈X\{\epsilon_a\}_{a\in X}{ϵa​}a∈X​.

Any function f:X→Cf:X\to \mathbb{C}f:X→C can be written as a linear combination of the basis vectors.

f(x)=∑a∈Xf(a)ϵa(x)f(x)=\sum_{a\in X} f(a)\epsilon_a(x)f(x)=a∈X∑​f(a)ϵa​(x)

Proof

This basically follows the same rascal as the previous proof. This time, the epsilon function only returns 111 when xj=ajx_j=a_jxj​=aj​ for all j=1,2,⋯ ,nj=1,2,\cdots,nj=1,2,⋯,n.

f(x)=∑a∈Xf(a)ϵa(x)=f(x)f(x)=\sum_{a\in X} f(a)\epsilon_a(x)=f(x)f(x)=a∈X∑​f(a)ϵa​(x)=f(x)

Definition of tensor product of basis elements

The tensor product of basis elements is defined by

ϵa≔ϵa1(1)⊗ϵa2(2)⊗⋯⊗ϵan(n)\epsilon_a\coloneqq\epsilon_{a_1}^{(1)}\otimes \epsilon_{a_2}^{(2)}\otimes \cdots \otimes \epsilon_{a_n}^{(n)}ϵa​:=ϵa1​(1)​⊗ϵa2​(2)​⊗⋯⊗ϵan​(n)​

This is a basis of H\mathscr{H}H, here H\mathscr{H}H is the set of all functions from X=X1×X2×⋯×XnX=X_1\times X_2\times \cdots \times X_nX=X1​×X2​×⋯×Xn​ to C\mathbb{C}C.

Definition of tensor product of two finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces

The tensor product of two finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces (in H\mathscr{H}H) is defined by

Let H1\mathscr{H}_1H1​ and H2\mathscr{H}_2H2​ be two finite dimensional Hilbert spaces. Let u1∈H1u_1\in \mathscr{H}_1u1​∈H1​ and v1∈H2v_1\in \mathscr{H}_2v1​∈H2​.

u1⊗v1u_1\otimes v_1u1​⊗v1​

is a bi-anti-linear map from H1×H2\mathscr{H}_1\times \mathscr{H}_2H1​×H2​ (the Cartesian product of H1\mathscr{H}_1H1​ and H2\mathscr{H}_2H2​, a tuple of two elements where first element is in H1\mathscr{H}_1H1​ and second element is in H2\mathscr{H}_2H2​) to F\mathbb{F}F (in this case, C\mathbb{C}C). And ∀u∈H1,v∈H2\forall u\in \mathscr{H}_1, v\in \mathscr{H}_2∀u∈H1​,v∈H2​,

(u1⊗v1)(u,v)=⟨u,u1⟩⟨v,v1⟩(u_1\otimes v_1)(u, v)=\langle u,u_1\rangle \langle v,v_1\rangle(u1​⊗v1​)(u,v)=⟨u,u1​⟩⟨v,v1​⟩

We call such forms decomposable. The tensor product of two finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, denoted by H1⊗H2\mathscr{H}_1\otimes \mathscr{H}_2H1​⊗H2​, is the set of all linear combinations of decomposable forms. Represented by the following:

(∑i=1naiui⊗vi)(u,v)≔∑i=1naj(uj⊗vj)(u,v)=∑i=1nai⟨v,ui⟩⟨vi,u⟩\left(\sum_{i=1}^n a_i u_i\otimes v_i\right)(u, v) \coloneqq \sum_{i=1}^n a_j(u_j\otimes v_j)(u,v)=\sum_{i=1}^n a_i \langle v,u_i\rangle \langle v_i,u\rangle(i=1∑n​ai​ui​⊗vi​)(u,v):=i=1∑n​aj​(uj​⊗vj​)(u,v)=i=1∑n​ai​⟨v,ui​⟩⟨vi​,u⟩

Note that ai∈Ca_i\in \mathbb{C}ai​∈C for complex-vector spaces.

This is a linear space of dimension dim⁡H1×dim⁡H2\dim \mathscr{H}_1\times \dim \mathscr{H}_2dimH1​×dimH2​.

We define the inner product of two elements of H1⊗H2\mathscr{H}_1\otimes \mathscr{H}_2H1​⊗H2​ (u1⊗v1:(H1⊗H2)→Cu_1\otimes v_1:(\mathscr{H}_1\otimes \mathscr{H}_2)\to \mathbb{C}u1​⊗v1​:(H1​⊗H2​)→C, u2⊗v2:(H1⊗H2)→Cu_2\otimes v_2:(\mathscr{H}_1\otimes \mathscr{H}_2)\to \mathbb{C}u2​⊗v2​:(H1​⊗H2​)→C ∈H1⊗H2\in \mathscr{H}_1\otimes \mathscr{H}_2∈H1​⊗H2​) by

⟨u1⊗v1,u2⊗v2⟩≔⟨u1,u2⟩⟨v1,v2⟩=(u1⊗v1)(u2,v2)\langle u_1\otimes v_1, u_2\otimes v_2\rangle\coloneqq\langle u_1,u_2\rangle \langle v_1,v_2\rangle=(u_1\otimes v_1)(u_2,v_2)⟨u1​⊗v1​,u2​⊗v2​⟩:=⟨u1​,u2​⟩⟨v1​,v2​⟩=(u1​⊗v1​)(u2​,v2​)

Tensor products of linear operators

Let T1T_1T1​ be a linear operator on H1\mathscr{H}_1H1​ and T2T_2T2​ be a linear operator on H2\mathscr{H}_2H2​, where H1\mathscr{H}_1H1​ and H2\mathscr{H}_2H2​ are finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces. The tensor product of T1T_1T1​ and T2T_2T2​ (denoted by T1⊗T2T_1\otimes T_2T1​⊗T2​) on H1⊗H2\mathscr{H}_1\otimes \mathscr{H}_2H1​⊗H2​, such that on decomposable elements is defined by

(T1⊗T2)(v1⊗v2)=T1(v1)⊗T2(v2)=⟨v1,T1(v1)⟩⟨v2,T2(v2)⟩(T_1\otimes T_2)(v_1\otimes v_2)=T_1(v_1)\otimes T_2(v_2)=\langle v_1,T_1(v_1)\rangle \langle v_2,T_2(v_2)\rangle(T1​⊗T2​)(v1​⊗v2​)=T1​(v1​)⊗T2​(v2​)=⟨v1​,T1​(v1​)⟩⟨v2​,T2​(v2​)⟩

for all v1∈H1v_1\in \mathscr{H}_1v1​∈H1​ and v2∈H2v_2\in \mathscr{H}_2v2​∈H2​.

The tensor product of two linear operators T1T_1T1​ and T2T_2T2​ is a linear combination in the form as follows:

∑i=1naiT1(ui)⊗T2(vi)\sum_{i=1}^n a_i T_1(u_i)\otimes T_2(v_i)i=1∑n​ai​T1​(ui​)⊗T2​(vi​)

for all ui∈H1u_i\in \mathscr{H}_1ui​∈H1​ and vi∈H2v_i\in \mathscr{H}_2vi​∈H2​.

Such tensor product of linear operators is well defined.

Proof

If ∑i=1naiui⊗vi=∑j=1mbjuj⊗vj\sum_{i=1}^n a_i u_i\otimes v_i=\sum_{j=1}^m b_j u_j\otimes v_j∑i=1n​ai​ui​⊗vi​=∑j=1m​bj​uj​⊗vj​, then ai=bja_i=b_jai​=bj​ for all i=1,2,⋯ ,ni=1,2,\cdots,ni=1,2,⋯,n and j=1,2,⋯ ,mj=1,2,\cdots,mj=1,2,⋯,m.

Then ∑i=1naiT1(ui)⊗T2(vi)=∑j=1mbjT1(uj)⊗T2(vj)\sum_{i=1}^n a_i T_1(u_i)\otimes T_2(v_i)=\sum_{j=1}^m b_j T_1(u_j)\otimes T_2(v_j)∑i=1n​ai​T1​(ui​)⊗T2​(vi​)=∑j=1m​bj​T1​(uj​)⊗T2​(vj​).

An example of

Tensor product of linear operators on Hilbert spaces

Let T1T_1T1​ be a linear operator on H1\mathscr{H}_1H1​ and T2T_2T2​ be a linear operator on H2\mathscr{H}_2H2​, where H1\mathscr{H}_1H1​ and H2\mathscr{H}_2H2​ are finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces. The tensor product of T1T_1T1​ and T2T_2T2​ (denoted by T1⊗T2T_1\otimes T_2T1​⊗T2​) on H1⊗H2\mathscr{H}_1\otimes \mathscr{H}_2H1​⊗H2​, such that on decomposable elements is defined by

(T1⊗T2)(v1⊗v2)=T1(v1)⊗T2(v2)=⟨v1,T1(v1)⟩⟨v2,T2(v2)⟩(T_1\otimes T_2)(v_1\otimes v_2)=T_1(v_1)\otimes T_2(v_2)=\langle v_1,T_1(v_1)\rangle \langle v_2,T_2(v_2)\rangle(T1​⊗T2​)(v1​⊗v2​)=T1​(v1​)⊗T2​(v2​)=⟨v1​,T1​(v1​)⟩⟨v2​,T2​(v2​)⟩

Extended Dirac notation

Suppose H=Cn\mathscr{H}=\mathbb{C}^nH=Cn with the standard basis {ei}\{e_i\}{ei​}.

ej=∣j⟩e_j=|j\rangleej​=∣j⟩ and

∣j1…jn⟩=ej1⊗ej2⊗⋯⊗ejn=|j_1\dots j_n\rangle=e_{j_1}\otimes e_{j_2}\otimes \cdots \otimes e_{j_n}=∣j1​…jn​⟩=ej1​​⊗ej2​​⊗⋯⊗ejn​​=

The Hadamard Transform

Let H=C2\mathscr{H}=\mathbb{C}^2H=C2 with the standard basis {e1,e2}={∣0⟩,∣1⟩}\{e_1,e_2\}=\{|0\rangle,|1\rangle\}{e1​,e2​}={∣0⟩,∣1⟩}.

The linear operator H2H_2H2​ is defined by

H2=12(111−1)=12(∣0⟩⟨0∣+∣1⟩⟨0∣+∣0⟩⟨1∣−∣1⟩⟨1∣)H_2=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|0\rangle\langle 0|+|1\rangle\langle 0|+|0\rangle\langle 1|-|1\rangle\langle 1|)H2​=2​1​(11​1−1​)=2​1​(∣0⟩⟨0∣+∣1⟩⟨0∣+∣0⟩⟨1∣−∣1⟩⟨1∣)

The Hadamard transform is the linear operator H2H_2H2​ on C2\mathbb{C}^2C2.

Singular value and Schmidt decomposition

Definition of SVD (Singular Value Decomposition)

Let T:U→VT:\mathscr{U}\to \mathscr{V}T:U→V be a linear operator between two finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces U\mathscr{U}U and V\mathscr{V}V.

We denote the inner product of U\mathscr{U}U and V\mathscr{V}V by ⟨⋅,⋅⟩\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle⟨⋅,⋅⟩.

Then there exists a decomposition of TTT

T=d1T1+d2T2+⋯+dnTnT=d_1 T_1+d_2 T_2+\cdots +d_n T_nT=d1​T1​+d2​T2​+⋯+dn​Tn​

with d1>d2>⋯>dn>0d_1>d_2>\cdots >d_n>0d1​>d2​>⋯>dn​>0 and Ti:U→VT_i:\mathscr{U}\to \mathscr{V}Ti​:U→V such that:

  1. TiTj∗=0T_iT_j^*=0Ti​Tj∗​=0, Ti∗Tj=0T_i^*T_j=0Ti∗​Tj​=0 for i≠ji\neq ji=j(
  2. Ti∣R(Ti∗):R(Ti∗)→R(Ti)T_i|_{\mathscr{R}(T_i^*)}:\mathscr{R}(T_i^*)\to \mathscr{R}(T_i)Ti​∣R(Ti∗​)​:R(Ti∗​)→R(Ti​) is an isomorphism with inverse Ti∗T_i^*Ti∗​ where R(⋅)\mathscr{R}(\cdot)R(⋅) is the range of the operator.

The did_idi​ are called the singular values of TTT.

Gram-Schmidt Decomposition 

Basic Group Theory

Finite groups

Definition of group

A group is a set GGG with a binary operation ⋅\cdot⋅ that satisfies the following axioms:

  1. Closure: For all a,b∈Ga,b\in Ga,b∈G, a⋅b∈Ga\cdot b\in Ga⋅b∈G.
  2. Associativity: For all a,b,c∈Ga,b,c\in Ga,b,c∈G, (a⋅b)⋅c=a⋅(b⋅c)(a\cdot b)\cdot c=a\cdot (b\cdot c)(a⋅b)⋅c=a⋅(b⋅c).
  3. Identity: There exists an element e∈Ge\in Ge∈G such that for all a∈Ga\in Ga∈G, a⋅e=e⋅a=aa\cdot e=e\cdot a=aa⋅e=e⋅a=a.
  4. Inverses: For all a∈Ga\in Ga∈G, there exists an element b∈Gb\in Gb∈G such that a⋅b=b⋅a=ea\cdot b=b\cdot a=ea⋅b=b⋅a=e.

Symmetric group SnS_nSn​

The symmetric group SnS_nSn​ is the group of all permutations of nnn elements.

Sn={f:{1,2,⋯ ,n}→{1,2,⋯ ,n} is a bijection}S_n=\{f: \{1,2,\cdots,n\}\to \{1,2,\cdots,n\} \text{ is a bijection}\}Sn​={f:{1,2,⋯,n}→{1,2,⋯,n} is a bijection}

Unitary group U(n)U(n)U(n)

The unitary group U(n)U(n)U(n) is the group of all n×nn\times nn×n unitary matrices.

Such that A∗=AA^*=AA∗=A, where A∗A^*A∗ is the complex conjugate transpose of AAA. A∗=(A‾)⊤A^*=(\overline{A})^\topA∗=(A)⊤.

Cyclic group Zn\mathbb{Z}_nZn​

The cyclic group Zn\mathbb{Z}_nZn​ is the group of all integers modulo nnn.

Zn={0,1,2,⋯ ,n−1}\mathbb{Z}_n=\{0,1,2,\cdots,n-1\}Zn​={0,1,2,⋯,n−1}

Definition of group homomorphism

A group homomorphism is a function Φ:G→H\varPhi:G\to HΦ:G→H between two groups GGG and HHH that satisfies the following axiom:

Φ(a⋅b)=Φ(a)⋅Φ(b)\varPhi(a\cdot b)=\varPhi(a)\cdot \varPhi(b)Φ(a⋅b)=Φ(a)⋅Φ(b)

A bijective group homomorphism is called group isomorphism.

Homomorphism sends identity to identity, inverses to inverses

Let Φ:G→H\varPhi:G\to HΦ:G→H be a group homomorphism. eGe_GeG​ and eHe_HeH​ are the identity elements of GGG and HHH respectively. Then

  1. Φ(eG)=eH\varPhi(e_G)=e_HΦ(eG​)=eH​
  2. Φ(a−1)=Φ(a)−1\varPhi(a^{-1})=\varPhi(a)^{-1}Φ(a−1)=Φ(a)−1. ∀a∈G\forall a\in G∀a∈G

More on the symmetric group

General linear group over C\mathbb{C}C

The general linear group over C\mathbb{C}C is the group of all n×nn\times nn×n invertible complex matrices.

GL(n,C)={A∈Mn(C) is invertible}GL(n,\mathbb{C})=\{A\in M_n(\mathbb{C}) \text{ is invertible}\}GL(n,C)={A∈Mn​(C) is invertible}

The map T:Sn→GL(n,C)T: S_n\to GL(n,\mathbb{C})T:Sn​→GL(n,C) is a group homomorphism.

Definition of sign of a permutation

Let T:Sn→GL(n,C)T:S_n\to GL(n,\mathbb{C})T:Sn​→GL(n,C) be the group homomorphism. The sign of a permutation σ∈Sn\sigma\in S_nσ∈Sn​ is defined by

sgn⁡(σ)=det⁡(T(σ))\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)=\det(T(\sigma))sgn(σ)=det(T(σ))

We say σ\sigmaσ is even if sgn⁡(σ)=1\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)=1sgn(σ)=1 and odd if sgn⁡(σ)=−1\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)=-1sgn(σ)=−1.

Fourier Transform in ZN\mathbb{Z}_NZN​.

The vector space L2(ZN)L^2(\mathbb{Z}_N)L2(ZN​) is the set of all complex-valued functions on ZN\mathbb{Z}_NZN​ with the inner product

⟨f,g⟩=∑k=0N−1f(k)‾g(k)\langle f,g\rangle=\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \overline{f(k)}g(k)⟨f,g⟩=k=0∑N−1​f(k)​g(k)

An orthonormal basis of L2(ZN)L^2(\mathbb{Z}_N)L2(ZN​) is given by δy,y∈ZN\delta_y,y\in \mathbb{Z}_Nδy​,y∈ZN​.

δy(k)={1if k=y0otherwise\delta_y(k)=\begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } k=y \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}δy​(k)={10​if k=yotherwise​

in Dirac notation, we have

δy=∣y⟩=∣y+N⟩\delta_y=|y\rangle=|y+N\rangleδy​=∣y⟩=∣y+N⟩

Definition of Fourier transform

Define φk(x)=1Ne2πikx/N\varphi_k(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}e^{2\pi i kx/N}φk​(x)=N​1​e2πikx/N for k∈ZNk\in \mathbb{Z}_Nk∈ZN​. φk:Z→C\varphi_k:\mathbb{Z}\to \mathbb{C}φk​:Z→C is a function.

The Fourier transform of a function F∈L2(ZN)F\in L^2(\mathbb{Z}_N)F∈L2(ZN​) such that (Ff)(k)=⟨φk,f⟩(Ff)(k)=\langle \varphi_k,f\rangle(Ff)(k)=⟨φk​,f⟩ is defined by

F=1N∑j=0N−1∑k=0N−1e2πikj/N∣k⟩⟨j∣F=\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\sum_{j=0}^{N-1} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{2\pi i kj/N}|k\rangle\langle j|F=N​1​j=0∑N−1​k=0∑N−1​e2πikj/N∣k⟩⟨j∣

Symmetric and anti-symmetric tensors

Let H⊗n\mathscr{H}^{\otimes n}H⊗n be the nnn-fold tensor product of a Hilbert space H\mathscr{H}H.

We define the SnS_nSn​ on H⊗n\mathscr{H}^{\otimes n}H⊗n by

Let η∈Sn\eta\in S_nη∈Sn​ be a permutation.

∏(η)v1⊗v2⊗⋯⊗vn=vη−1(1)⊗vη−1(2)⊗⋯⊗vη−1(n)\prod(\eta)v_1\otimes v_2\otimes \cdots \otimes v_n=v_{\eta^{-1}(1)}\otimes v_{\eta^{-1}(2)}\otimes \cdots \otimes v_{\eta^{-1}(n)}∏(η)v1​⊗v2​⊗⋯⊗vn​=vη−1(1)​⊗vη−1(2)​⊗⋯⊗vη−1(n)​

And extend to H⊗n\mathscr{H}^{\otimes n}H⊗n by linearity.

This gives the property that ζ,η∈Sn\zeta,\eta\in S_nζ,η∈Sn​, ∏(ζη)=∏(ζ)∏(η)\prod(\zeta\eta)=\prod(\zeta)\prod(\eta)∏(ζη)=∏(ζ)∏(η).

Definition of symmetric and anti-symmetric tensors

Let H\mathscr{H}H be a finite-dimensional Hilbert space.

An element in H⊗n\mathscr{H}^{\otimes n}H⊗n is called symmetric if it is invariant under the action of SnS_nSn​. Let α∈H⊗n\alpha\in \mathscr{H}^{\otimes n}α∈H⊗n

∏(η)α=α for all η∈Sn.\prod(\eta)\alpha=\alpha \text{ for all } \eta\in S_n.∏(η)α=α for all η∈Sn​.

It is called anti-symmetric if

∏(η)α=sgn⁡(η)α for all η∈Sn.\prod(\eta)\alpha=\operatorname{sgn}(\eta)\alpha \text{ for all } \eta\in S_n.∏(η)α=sgn(η)α for all η∈Sn​.
